title: Security+ SY0-701 — Section 23: Governance, Risk & Compliance
description: Jason Dion Security+ SY0-701 — Section 23: Governance, Risk and Compliance (GRC). Domain: Security Program Management & Oversight (5.0).

questions.question: What does GRC stand for and what does it encompass?
answers: Governance, Risk Management, and Compliance — a framework for organizational security management
General Risk Controls — a security checklist
Global Regulatory Compliance — international security standards
Government Response Capabilities — incident reporting framework
questions.explanation: GRC integrates Governance (policies and accountability), Risk Management (identifying and mitigating risks), and Compliance (meeting regulatory requirements) into a unified approach.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating risk?
answers: Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Asset Value (or Risk = Likelihood × Impact)
Risk = Cost of Control / Probability
Risk = Vulnerabilities / Threats
Risk = Incidents / Year × Cost per Incident
questions.explanation: Risk is commonly expressed as the product of likelihood (threat exploiting a vulnerability) and impact (damage to the organization). The fuller formula incorporates asset value.

questions.question: What are the four main risk treatment options?
answers: Accept, Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate
Ignore, Document, Report, Fix
Detect, Prevent, Respond, Recover
Assess, Plan, Implement, Monitor
questions.explanation: Organizations can Accept (tolerate) risk, Avoid (eliminate the risky activity), Transfer (insurance, outsourcing), or Mitigate (implement controls to reduce risk).

questions.question: What is the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)?
answers: A voluntary framework of standards to help organizations manage cybersecurity risk
A mandatory US government regulation
A technical standard for encryption
A certification program for security professionals
questions.explanation: The NIST CSF provides a common language and systematic approach to managing cybersecurity risk, organized around five functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.

questions.question: What regulation governs the protection of healthcare data in the US?
answers: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)
PCI DSS
GDPR
SOX
questions.explanation: HIPAA establishes standards for protecting Protected Health Information (PHI) and applies to healthcare providers, insurers, and their business associates.

questions.question: What does PCI DSS require organizations to do?
answers: Protect cardholder data through a set of security standards for payment processing
Comply with US government data classification rules
Encrypt all corporate communications
Conduct annual penetration tests only
questions.explanation: PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard) requires organizations that process payment cards to implement security controls to protect cardholder data.

questions.question: What is the geographic scope of GDPR?
answers: Applies to any organization processing EU residents' data, regardless of where the org is located
Applies only to EU-based organizations
Applies only to US organizations doing business in Europe
Applies only to data stored in EU data centers
questions.explanation: GDPR has extraterritorial scope — any organization worldwide that processes personal data of EU residents must comply, regardless of the organization's location.

questions.question: What is the difference between a security policy and a security procedure?
answers: Policy states what must be done (high-level); procedures describe how to do it (step-by-step)
Policies are technical documents; procedures are management documents
Policies are optional; procedures are mandatory
They are the same type of document
questions.explanation: Policies are high-level governance statements defining what is required. Procedures are detailed operational instructions on how to implement policy requirements.

questions.question: What is the difference between an internal and external security audit?
answers: Internal audits are conducted by employees; external audits by independent third parties
Internal audits are more rigorous than external audits
External audits are conducted by government regulators only
They differ only in cost
questions.explanation: Internal audits are performed by the organization's own staff to self-assess controls. External audits use independent third parties for greater objectivity and credibility.

questions.question: What is the difference between due care and due diligence in security?
answers: Due diligence is identifying risks; due care is acting on those risks with reasonable controls
Due care is done before; due diligence is done after an incident
They are the same concept
Due diligence is technical; due care is physical
questions.explanation: Due diligence is the ongoing effort to understand security risks. Due care is taking reasonable actions to protect against those risks. Together they demonstrate responsible management.

questions.question: What is a risk register?
answers: A documented list of identified risks, their assessment, and treatment status
A log of security incidents
A list of security controls in place
A compliance checklist
questions.explanation: A risk register tracks identified risks including their description, likelihood, impact, risk owner, treatment approach, and current status — a central tool for risk management.

questions.question: What is third-party risk management?
answers: Assessing and managing security risks introduced by vendors, suppliers, and partners
Managing risks in three-tiered network architectures
Conducting penetration tests against partner organizations
Sharing threat intelligence with third parties
questions.explanation: Third-party risk management evaluates the security posture of vendors and partners who have access to your systems or data, ensuring they meet your security standards.

questions.question: What does ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy) calculate?
answers: ALE = SLE × ARO (Single Loss Expectancy × Annualized Rate of Occurrence)
ALE = Asset Value × Threat Level
ALE = Control Cost / Risk Reduction
ALE = Incident Count × Average Downtime
questions.explanation: ALE measures the expected annual cost of a specific risk. SLE is the loss from one occurrence; ARO is how often it occurs per year. Together they quantify financial risk.

questions.question: What is ISO/IEC 27001?
answers: An international standard for establishing and managing an Information Security Management System (ISMS)
A US government security framework
A certification for security professionals
A technical standard for network security
questions.explanation: ISO 27001 is an internationally recognized standard that specifies requirements for an ISMS — a systematic approach to managing sensitive information to remain secure.

questions.question: What is separation of duties and why is it important?
answers: Dividing critical tasks among multiple people to prevent fraud and errors
Separating security duties from IT duties
Having different passwords for different systems
Isolating security personnel from other employees
questions.explanation: Separation of duties ensures no single person has complete control over a critical process, reducing insider fraud risk (e.g., requiring two approvals for financial transactions).
